lines: If it be love indeed, tell me how much.
fullText: If it be love indeed, tell me how much.

lines: I'll set a bourn how far to be beloved.
fullText: I'll set a bourn how far to be beloved.

lines: Nay, hear them, Antony.
Fulvia perchance is angry. Or who knows
If the scarce-bearded Caesar have not sent
His powerful mandate to you: "Do this, or this;
Take in that kingdom, and enfranchise that.
Perform 't, or else we damn thee."
fullText: Nay, hear them, Antony. Fulvia perchance is angry. Or who knows If the scarce-bearded Caesar have not sent His powerful mandate to you: "Do this, or this; Take in that kingdom, and enfranchise that. Perform 't, or else we damn thee."

lines: Perchance? Nay, and most like.
You must not stay here longer; your dismission
Is come from Caesar. Therefore hear it, Antony.
Where's Fulvia's process? Caesar's, I would say--
both?
Call in the messengers. As I am Egypt's queen,
Thou blushest, Antony, and that blood of thine
Is Caesar's homager; else so thy cheek pays shame
When shrill-tongued Fulvia scolds. The messengers!
fullText: Perchance? Nay, and most like. You must not stay here longer; your dismission Is come from Caesar. Therefore hear it, Antony. Where's Fulvia's process? Caesar's, I would say-- both? Call in the messengers. As I am Egypt's queen, Thou blushest, Antony, and that blood of thine Is Caesar's homager; else so thy cheek pays shame When shrill-tongued Fulvia scolds. The messengers!

lines: Excellent falsehood!
Why did he marry Fulvia and not love her?
I'll seem the fool I am not. Antony
Will be himself.
fullText: Excellent falsehood! Why did he marry Fulvia and not love her? I'll seem the fool I am not. Antony Will be himself.

lines: Hear the ambassadors.
fullText: Hear the ambassadors.

lines: Saw you my lord?
fullText: Saw you my lord?

lines: Was he not here?
fullText: Was he not here?

lines: He was disposed to mirth, but on the sudden
A Roman thought hath struck him.--Enobarbus!
fullText: He was disposed to mirth, but on the sudden A Roman thought hath struck him.--Enobarbus!

lines: Seek him and bring him hither.--Where's Alexas?
fullText: Seek him and bring him hither.--Where's Alexas?

lines: We will not look upon him. Go with us.
fullText: We will not look upon him. Go with us.

lines: Where is he?
fullText: Where is he?

lines: See where he is, who's with him, what he does.
I did not send you. If you find him sad,
Say I am dancing; if in mirth, report
That I am sudden sick. Quick, and return.
fullText: See where he is, who's with him, what he does. I did not send you. If you find him sad, Say I am dancing; if in mirth, report That I am sudden sick. Quick, and return.

lines: What should I do I do not?
fullText: What should I do I do not?

lines: Thou teachest like a fool: the way to lose him.
fullText: Thou teachest like a fool: the way to lose him.

lines: I am sick and sullen.
fullText: I am sick and sullen.

lines: Help me away, dear Charmian! I shall fall.
It cannot be thus long; the sides of nature
Will not sustain it.
fullText: Help me away, dear Charmian! I shall fall. It cannot be thus long; the sides of nature Will not sustain it.

lines: Pray you stand farther from me.
fullText: Pray you stand farther from me.

lines: I know by that same eye there's some good news.
What, says the married woman you may go?
Would she had never given you leave to come.
Let her not say 'tis I that keep you here.
I have no power upon you. Hers you are.
fullText: I know by that same eye there's some good news. What, says the married woman you may go? Would she had never given you leave to come. Let her not say 'tis I that keep you here. I have no power upon you. Hers you are.

lines: O, never was there queen
So mightily betrayed! Yet at the first
I saw the treasons planted.
fullText: O, never was there queen So mightily betrayed! Yet at the first I saw the treasons planted.

lines: Why should I think you can be mine, and true--
Though you in swearing shake the throned gods--
Who have been false to Fulvia? Riotous madness,
To be entangled with those mouth-made vows
Which break themselves in swearing!
fullText: Why should I think you can be mine, and true-- Though you in swearing shake the throned gods-- Who have been false to Fulvia? Riotous madness, To be entangled with those mouth-made vows Which break themselves in swearing!

lines: Nay, pray you seek no color for your going,
But bid farewell and go. When you sued staying,
Then was the time for words. No going then!
Eternity was in our lips and eyes,
Bliss in our brows' bent; none our parts so poor
But was a race of heaven. They are so still,
Or thou, the greatest soldier of the world,
Art turned the greatest liar.
fullText: Nay, pray you seek no color for your going, But bid farewell and go. When you sued staying, Then was the time for words. No going then! Eternity was in our lips and eyes, Bliss in our brows' bent; none our parts so poor But was a race of heaven. They are so still, Or thou, the greatest soldier of the world, Art turned the greatest liar.

lines: I would I had thy inches. Thou shouldst know
There were a heart in Egypt.
fullText: I would I had thy inches. Thou shouldst know There were a heart in Egypt.

lines: Though age from folly could not give me freedom,
It does from childishness. Can Fulvia die?
fullText: Though age from folly could not give me freedom, It does from childishness. Can Fulvia die?

lines: O, most false love!
Where be the sacred vials thou shouldst fill
With sorrowful water? Now I see, I see,
In Fulvia's death, how mine received shall be.
fullText: O, most false love! Where be the sacred vials thou shouldst fill With sorrowful water? Now I see, I see, In Fulvia's death, how mine received shall be.

lines: Cut my lace, Charmian, come!
But let it be; I am quickly ill and well;
So Antony loves.
fullText: Cut my lace, Charmian, come! But let it be; I am quickly ill and well; So Antony loves.

lines: So Fulvia told me.
I prithee turn aside and weep for her,
Then bid adieu to me, and say the tears
Belong to Egypt. Good now, play one scene
Of excellent dissembling, and let it look
Like perfect honor.
fullText: So Fulvia told me. I prithee turn aside and weep for her, Then bid adieu to me, and say the tears Belong to Egypt. Good now, play one scene Of excellent dissembling, and let it look Like perfect honor.

lines: You can do better yet, but this is meetly.
fullText: You can do better yet, but this is meetly.

lines: And target. Still he mends.
But this is not the best. Look, prithee, Charmian,
How this Herculean Roman does become
The carriage of his chafe.
fullText: And target. Still he mends. But this is not the best. Look, prithee, Charmian, How this Herculean Roman does become The carriage of his chafe.

lines: Courteous lord, one word.
Sir, you and I must part, but that's not it;
Sir, you and I have loved, but there's not it;
That you know well. Something it is I would--
O, my oblivion is a very Antony,
And I am all forgotten.
fullText: Courteous lord, one word. Sir, you and I must part, but that's not it; Sir, you and I have loved, but there's not it; That you know well. Something it is I would-- O, my oblivion is a very Antony, And I am all forgotten.

lines: 'Tis sweating labor
To bear such idleness so near the heart
As Cleopatra this. But, sir, forgive me,
Since my becomings kill me when they do not
Eye well to you. Your honor calls you hence;
Therefore be deaf to my unpitied folly,
And all the gods go with you. Upon your sword
Sit laurel victory, and smooth success
Be strewed before your feet.
fullText: 'Tis sweating labor To bear such idleness so near the heart As Cleopatra this. But, sir, forgive me, Since my becomings kill me when they do not Eye well to you. Your honor calls you hence; Therefore be deaf to my unpitied folly, And all the gods go with you. Upon your sword Sit laurel victory, and smooth success Be strewed before your feet.

lines: Charmian!
fullText: Charmian!

lines: Ha, ha! Give me to drink mandragora.
fullText: Ha, ha! Give me to drink mandragora.

lines: That I might sleep out this great gap of time
My Antony is away.
fullText: That I might sleep out this great gap of time My Antony is away.

lines: O, 'tis treason!
fullText: O, 'tis treason!

lines: Thou, eunuch Mardian!
fullText: Thou, eunuch Mardian!

lines: Not now to hear thee sing. I take no pleasure
In aught an eunuch has. 'Tis well for thee
That, being unseminared, thy freer thoughts
May not fly forth of Egypt. Hast thou affections?
fullText: Not now to hear thee sing. I take no pleasure In aught an eunuch has. 'Tis well for thee That, being unseminared, thy freer thoughts May not fly forth of Egypt. Hast thou affections?

lines: Indeed?
fullText: Indeed?

lines: O, Charmian,
Where think'st thou he is now? Stands he, or sits he?
Or does he walk? Or is he on his horse?
O happy horse, to bear the weight of Antony!
Do bravely, horse, for wot'st thou whom thou
mov'st?
The demi-Atlas of this Earth, the arm
And burgonet of men. He's speaking now,
Or murmuring "Where's my serpent of old Nile?"
For so he calls me. Now I feed myself
With most delicious poison. Think on me
That am with Phoebus' amorous pinches black,
And wrinkled deep in time? Broad-fronted Caesar,
When thou wast here above the ground, I was
A morsel for a monarch. And great Pompey
Would stand and make his eyes grow in my brow;
There would he anchor his aspect, and die
With looking on his life.
fullText: O, Charmian, Where think'st thou he is now? Stands he, or sits he? Or does he walk? Or is he on his horse? O happy horse, to bear the weight of Antony! Do bravely, horse, for wot'st thou whom thou mov'st? The demi-Atlas of this Earth, the arm And burgonet of men. He's speaking now, Or murmuring "Where's my serpent of old Nile?" For so he calls me. Now I feed myself With most delicious poison. Think on me That am with Phoebus' amorous pinches black, And wrinkled deep in time? Broad-fronted Caesar, When thou wast here above the ground, I was A morsel for a monarch. And great Pompey Would stand and make his eyes grow in my brow; There would he anchor his aspect, and die With looking on his life.

lines: How much unlike art thou Mark Antony!
Yet coming from him, that great med'cine hath
With his tinct gilded thee.
How goes it with my brave Mark Antony?
fullText: How much unlike art thou Mark Antony! Yet coming from him, that great med'cine hath With his tinct gilded thee. How goes it with my brave Mark Antony?

lines: Mine ear must pluck it thence.
fullText: Mine ear must pluck it thence.

lines: What, was he sad, or merry?
fullText: What, was he sad, or merry?

lines: O, well-divided disposition!--Note him,
Note him, good Charmian, 'tis the man! But note
him:
He was not sad, for he would shine on those
That make their looks by his; he was not merry,
Which seemed to tell them his remembrance lay
In Egypt with his joy; but between both.
O, heavenly mingle!--Be'st thou sad or merry,
The violence of either thee becomes,
So does it no man's else.--Met'st thou my posts?
fullText: O, well-divided disposition!--Note him, Note him, good Charmian, 'tis the man! But note him: He was not sad, for he would shine on those That make their looks by his; he was not merry, Which seemed to tell them his remembrance lay In Egypt with his joy; but between both. O, heavenly mingle!--Be'st thou sad or merry, The violence of either thee becomes, So does it no man's else.--Met'st thou my posts?

lines: Who's born that day
When I forget to send to Antony
Shall die a beggar.--Ink and paper, Charmian.--
Welcome, my good Alexas.--Did I, Charmian,
Ever love Caesar so?
fullText: Who's born that day When I forget to send to Antony Shall die a beggar.--Ink and paper, Charmian.-- Welcome, my good Alexas.--Did I, Charmian, Ever love Caesar so?

lines: Be choked with such another emphasis!
Say "the brave Antony."
fullText: Be choked with such another emphasis! Say "the brave Antony."

lines: By Isis, I will give thee bloody teeth
If thou with Caesar paragon again
My man of men.
fullText: By Isis, I will give thee bloody teeth If thou with Caesar paragon again My man of men.

lines: My salad days,
When I was green in judgment, cold in blood,
To say as I said then. But come, away,
Get me ink and paper.
He shall have every day a several greeting,
Or I'll unpeople Egypt.
fullText: My salad days, When I was green in judgment, cold in blood, To say as I said then. But come, away, Get me ink and paper. He shall have every day a several greeting, Or I'll unpeople Egypt.

lines: Give me some music--music, moody food
Of us that trade in love.
fullText: Give me some music--music, moody food Of us that trade in love.

lines: Let it alone. Let's to billiards. Come, Charmian.
fullText: Let it alone. Let's to billiards. Come, Charmian.

lines: As well a woman with an eunuch played
As with a woman.--Come, you'll play with me, sir?
fullText: As well a woman with an eunuch played As with a woman.--Come, you'll play with me, sir?

lines: And when good will is showed, though 't come too
short,
The actor may plead pardon. I'll none now.
Give me mine angle; we'll to th' river. There,
My music playing far off, I will betray
Tawny-finned fishes. My bended hook shall pierce
Their slimy jaws, and as I draw them up
I'll think them every one an Antony
And say "Aha! You're caught."
fullText: And when good will is showed, though 't come too short, The actor may plead pardon. I'll none now. Give me mine angle; we'll to th' river. There, My music playing far off, I will betray Tawny-finned fishes. My bended hook shall pierce Their slimy jaws, and as I draw them up I'll think them every one an Antony And say "Aha! You're caught."

lines: That time?--O, times!--
I laughed him out of patience; and that night
I laughed him into patience; and next morn,
Ere the ninth hour, I drunk him to his bed,
Then put my tires and mantles on him, whilst
I wore his sword Philippan.
fullText: That time?--O, times!-- I laughed him out of patience; and that night I laughed him into patience; and next morn, Ere the ninth hour, I drunk him to his bed, Then put my tires and mantles on him, whilst I wore his sword Philippan.

lines: Antonio's dead! If thou say so, villain,
Thou kill'st thy mistress. But well and free,
If thou so yield him, there is gold, and here
My bluest veins to kiss, a hand that kings
Have lipped and trembled kissing.
fullText: Antonio's dead! If thou say so, villain, Thou kill'st thy mistress. But well and free, If thou so yield him, there is gold, and here My bluest veins to kiss, a hand that kings Have lipped and trembled kissing.

lines: Why, there's more gold. But sirrah, mark, we use
To say the dead are well. Bring it to that,
The gold I give thee will I melt and pour
Down thy ill-uttering throat.
fullText: Why, there's more gold. But sirrah, mark, we use To say the dead are well. Bring it to that, The gold I give thee will I melt and pour Down thy ill-uttering throat.

lines: Well, go to, I will.
But there's no goodness in thy face--if Antony
Be free and healthful, so tart a favor
To trumpet such good tidings! If not well,
Thou shouldst come like a Fury crowned with snakes,
Not like a formal man.
fullText: Well, go to, I will. But there's no goodness in thy face--if Antony Be free and healthful, so tart a favor To trumpet such good tidings! If not well, Thou shouldst come like a Fury crowned with snakes, Not like a formal man.

lines: I have a mind to strike thee ere thou speak'st.
Yet if thou say Antony lives, is well,
Or friends with Caesar or not captive to him,
I'll set thee in a shower of gold and hail
Rich pearls upon thee.
fullText: I have a mind to strike thee ere thou speak'st. Yet if thou say Antony lives, is well, Or friends with Caesar or not captive to him, I'll set thee in a shower of gold and hail Rich pearls upon thee.

lines: Well said.
fullText: Well said.

lines: Th' art an honest man.
fullText: Th' art an honest man.

lines: Make thee a fortune from me.
fullText: Make thee a fortune from me.

lines: I do not like "But yet." It does allay
The good precedence. Fie upon "But yet."
"But yet" is as a jailer to bring forth
Some monstrous malefactor. Prithee, friend,
Pour out the pack of matter to mine ear,
The good and bad together: he's friends with Caesar,
In state of health, thou say'st, and, thou say'st, free.
fullText: I do not like "But yet." It does allay The good precedence. Fie upon "But yet." "But yet" is as a jailer to bring forth Some monstrous malefactor. Prithee, friend, Pour out the pack of matter to mine ear, The good and bad together: he's friends with Caesar, In state of health, thou say'st, and, thou say'st, free.

lines: For what good turn?
fullText: For what good turn?

lines: I am pale, Charmian.
fullText: I am pale, Charmian.

lines: The most infectious pestilence upon thee!
fullText: The most infectious pestilence upon thee!

lines: What say you?
Hence, horrible villain, or I'll spurn thine eyes
Like balls before me! I'll unhair thy head!
Thou shalt be whipped with wire and stewed in
brine,
Smarting in ling'ring pickle.
fullText: What say you? Hence, horrible villain, or I'll spurn thine eyes Like balls before me! I'll unhair thy head! Thou shalt be whipped with wire and stewed in brine, Smarting in ling'ring pickle.

lines: Say 'tis not so, a province I will give thee
And make thy fortunes proud. The blow thou hadst
Shall make thy peace for moving me to rage,
And I will boot thee with what gift beside
Thy modesty can beg.
fullText: Say 'tis not so, a province I will give thee And make thy fortunes proud. The blow thou hadst Shall make thy peace for moving me to rage, And I will boot thee with what gift beside Thy modesty can beg.

lines: Rogue, thou hast lived too long.
fullText: Rogue, thou hast lived too long.

lines: Some innocents 'scape not the thunderbolt.
Melt Egypt into Nile, and kindly creatures
Turn all to serpents! Call the slave again.
Though I am mad, I will not bite him. Call!
fullText: Some innocents 'scape not the thunderbolt. Melt Egypt into Nile, and kindly creatures Turn all to serpents! Call the slave again. Though I am mad, I will not bite him. Call!

lines: I will not hurt him.
These hands do lack nobility that they strike
A meaner than myself, since I myself
Have given myself the cause.
fullText: I will not hurt him. These hands do lack nobility that they strike A meaner than myself, since I myself Have given myself the cause.

lines: Is he married?
I cannot hate thee worser than I do
If thou again say "yes."
fullText: Is he married? I cannot hate thee worser than I do If thou again say "yes."

lines: The gods confound thee! Dost thou hold there still?
fullText: The gods confound thee! Dost thou hold there still?

lines: O, I would thou didst,
So half my Egypt were submerged and made
A cistern for scaled snakes! Go, get thee hence.
Hadst thou Narcissus in thy face, to me
Thou wouldst appear most ugly. He is married?
fullText: O, I would thou didst, So half my Egypt were submerged and made A cistern for scaled snakes! Go, get thee hence. Hadst thou Narcissus in thy face, to me Thou wouldst appear most ugly. He is married?

lines: He is married?
fullText: He is married?

lines: O, that his fault should make a knave of thee
That art not what th' art sure of! Get thee hence.
The merchandise which thou hast brought from
Rome
Are all too dear for me. Lie they upon thy hand,
And be undone by 'em!
fullText: O, that his fault should make a knave of thee That art not what th' art sure of! Get thee hence. The merchandise which thou hast brought from Rome Are all too dear for me. Lie they upon thy hand, And be undone by 'em!

lines: In praising Antony, I have dispraised Caesar.
fullText: In praising Antony, I have dispraised Caesar.

lines: I am paid for 't now. Lead me from hence;
I faint. O, Iras, Charmian! 'Tis no matter.--
Go to the fellow, good Alexas. Bid him
Report the feature of Octavia, her years,
Her inclination; let him not leave out
The color of her hair. Bring me word quickly.
Let him forever go--let him not, Charmian.
Though he be painted one way like a Gorgon,
The other way 's a Mars. Bid you
Alexas
Bring me word how tall she is.--Pity me,
Charmian,
But do not speak to me. Lead me to my chamber.
fullText: I am paid for 't now. Lead me from hence; I faint. O, Iras, Charmian! 'Tis no matter.-- Go to the fellow, good Alexas. Bid him Report the feature of Octavia, her years, Her inclination; let him not leave out The color of her hair. Bring me word quickly. Let him forever go--let him not, Charmian. Though he be painted one way like a Gorgon, The other way 's a Mars. Bid you Alexas Bring me word how tall she is.--Pity me, Charmian, But do not speak to me. Lead me to my chamber.

lines: Where is the fellow?
fullText: Where is the fellow?

lines: Go to, go to.--Come hither, sir.
fullText: Go to, go to.--Come hither, sir.

lines: That Herod's head
I'll have! But how, when Antony is gone,
Through whom I might command it?--Come thou
near.
fullText: That Herod's head I'll have! But how, when Antony is gone, Through whom I might command it?--Come thou near.

lines: Did'st thou behold Octavia?
fullText: Did'st thou behold Octavia?

lines: Where?
fullText: Where?

lines: Is she as tall as me?
fullText: Is she as tall as me?

lines: Didst hear her speak? Is she shrill-tongued or low?
fullText: Didst hear her speak? Is she shrill-tongued or low?

lines: That's not so good. He cannot like her long.
fullText: That's not so good. He cannot like her long.

lines: I think so, Charmian: dull of tongue, and
dwarfish!--
What majesty is in her gait? Remember,
If e'er thou looked'st on majesty.
fullText: I think so, Charmian: dull of tongue, and dwarfish!-- What majesty is in her gait? Remember, If e'er thou looked'st on majesty.

lines: Is this certain?
fullText: Is this certain?

lines: He's very knowing.
I do perceive 't. There's nothing in her yet.
The fellow has good judgment.
fullText: He's very knowing. I do perceive 't. There's nothing in her yet. The fellow has good judgment.

lines: Guess at her years, I
prithee.
fullText: Guess at her years, I prithee.

lines: Widow? Charmian, hark.
fullText: Widow? Charmian, hark.

lines: Bear'st thou her face in mind? Is 't long or round?
fullText: Bear'st thou her face in mind? Is 't long or round?

lines: For the most part, too, they are foolish that are so.
Her hair what color?
fullText: For the most part, too, they are foolish that are so. Her hair what color?

lines: There's gold for thee.
Thou must not take my former sharpness ill.
I will employ thee back again. I find thee
Most fit for business. Go, make thee ready.
Our letters are prepared.
fullText: There's gold for thee. Thou must not take my former sharpness ill. I will employ thee back again. I find thee Most fit for business. Go, make thee ready. Our letters are prepared.

lines: Indeed he is so. I repent me much
That so I harried him. Why, methinks, by him,
This creature's no such thing.
fullText: Indeed he is so. I repent me much That so I harried him. Why, methinks, by him, This creature's no such thing.

lines: The man hath seen some majesty, and should know.
fullText: The man hath seen some majesty, and should know.

lines: I have one thing more to ask him yet, good
Charmian,
But 'tis no matter. Thou shalt bring him to me
Where I will write. All may be well enough.
fullText: I have one thing more to ask him yet, good Charmian, But 'tis no matter. Thou shalt bring him to me Where I will write. All may be well enough.

lines: I will be even with thee, doubt it not.
fullText: I will be even with thee, doubt it not.

lines: Thou hast forspoke my being in these wars
And say'st it is not fit.
fullText: Thou hast forspoke my being in these wars And say'st it is not fit.

lines: Is 't not denounced against us? Why should not we
Be there in person?
fullText: Is 't not denounced against us? Why should not we Be there in person?

lines: What is 't you say?
fullText: What is 't you say?

lines: Sink Rome, and their tongues rot
That speak against us! A charge we bear i' th' war,
And as the president of my kingdom will
Appear there for a man. Speak not against it.
I will not stay behind.
fullText: Sink Rome, and their tongues rot That speak against us! A charge we bear i' th' war, And as the president of my kingdom will Appear there for a man. Speak not against it. I will not stay behind.

lines: Celerity is never more admired
Than by the negligent.
fullText: Celerity is never more admired Than by the negligent.

lines: By sea, what else?
fullText: By sea, what else?

lines: I have sixty sails, Caesar none better.
fullText: I have sixty sails, Caesar none better.

lines: Let me sit down. O Juno!
fullText: Let me sit down. O Juno!

lines: Ah, stand by.
fullText: Ah, stand by.

lines: Well, then, sustain me. O!
fullText: Well, then, sustain me. O!

lines: O, my lord, my lord,
Forgive my fearful sails! I little thought
You would have followed.
fullText: O, my lord, my lord, Forgive my fearful sails! I little thought You would have followed.

lines: O, my pardon!
fullText: O, my pardon!

lines: Pardon, pardon!
fullText: Pardon, pardon!

lines: What shall we do, Enobarbus?
fullText: What shall we do, Enobarbus?

lines: Is Antony or we in fault for this?
fullText: Is Antony or we in fault for this?

lines: Prithee, peace.
fullText: Prithee, peace.

lines: That head, my lord?
fullText: That head, my lord?

lines: What, no more ceremony? See, my women,
Against the blown rose may they stop their nose
That kneeled unto the buds.--Admit him, sir.
fullText: What, no more ceremony? See, my women, Against the blown rose may they stop their nose That kneeled unto the buds.--Admit him, sir.

lines: Caesar's will?
fullText: Caesar's will?

lines: None but friends. Say boldly.
fullText: None but friends. Say boldly.

lines: Go on; right royal.
fullText: Go on; right royal.

lines: O!
fullText: O!

lines: He is a god and knows
What is most right. Mine honor was not yielded,
But conquered merely.
fullText: He is a god and knows What is most right. Mine honor was not yielded, But conquered merely.

lines: What's your name?
fullText: What's your name?

lines: Most kind messenger,
Say to great Caesar this in deputation:
I kiss his conqu'ring hand. Tell him I am prompt
To lay my crown at 's feet, and there to kneel.
Tell him, from his all-obeying breath I hear
The doom of Egypt.
fullText: Most kind messenger, Say to great Caesar this in deputation: I kiss his conqu'ring hand. Tell him I am prompt To lay my crown at 's feet, and there to kneel. Tell him, from his all-obeying breath I hear The doom of Egypt.

lines: Your Caesar's father oft,
When he hath mused of taking kingdoms in,
Bestowed his lips on that unworthy place
As it rained kisses.
fullText: Your Caesar's father oft, When he hath mused of taking kingdoms in, Bestowed his lips on that unworthy place As it rained kisses.

lines: Good my lord--
fullText: Good my lord--

lines: O, is 't come to this?
fullText: O, is 't come to this?

lines: Wherefore is this?
fullText: Wherefore is this?

lines: Have you done yet?
fullText: Have you done yet?

lines: I must stay his time.
fullText: I must stay his time.

lines: Not know me yet?
fullText: Not know me yet?

lines: Ah, dear, if I be so,
From my cold heart let heaven engender hail
And poison it in the source, and the first stone
Drop in my neck; as it determines, so
Dissolve my life! The next Caesarion smite,
Till by degrees the memory of my womb,
Together with my brave Egyptians all,
By the discandying of this pelleted storm
Lie graveless till the flies and gnats of Nile
Have buried them for prey!
fullText: Ah, dear, if I be so, From my cold heart let heaven engender hail And poison it in the source, and the first stone Drop in my neck; as it determines, so Dissolve my life! The next Caesarion smite, Till by degrees the memory of my womb, Together with my brave Egyptians all, By the discandying of this pelleted storm Lie graveless till the flies and gnats of Nile Have buried them for prey!

lines: That's my brave lord!
fullText: That's my brave lord!

lines: It is my birthday.
I had thought t' have held it poor. But since my lord
Is Antony again, I will be Cleopatra.
fullText: It is my birthday. I had thought t' have held it poor. But since my lord Is Antony again, I will be Cleopatra.

lines: Call all his noble captains to my lord.
fullText: Call all his noble captains to my lord.

lines: What means this?
fullText: What means this?

lines: What does he mean?
fullText: What does he mean?

lines: Sleep a little.
fullText: Sleep a little.

lines: Nay, I'll help too.
What's this for?
fullText: Nay, I'll help too. What's this for?

lines: Sooth, la, I'll help. Thus it must be.
fullText: Sooth, la, I'll help. Thus it must be.

lines: Is not this buckled well?
fullText: Is not this buckled well?

lines: Lead me.
He goes forth gallantly. That he and Caesar might
Determine this great war in single fight,
Then Antony--but now--. Well, on.
fullText: Lead me. He goes forth gallantly. That he and Caesar might Determine this great war in single fight, Then Antony--but now--. Well, on.

lines: Lord of lords!
O infinite virtue, com'st thou smiling from
The world's great snare uncaught?
fullText: Lord of lords! O infinite virtue, com'st thou smiling from The world's great snare uncaught?

lines: I'll give thee, friend,
An armor all of gold. It was a king's.
fullText: I'll give thee, friend, An armor all of gold. It was a king's.

lines: Why is my lord enraged against his love?
fullText: Why is my lord enraged against his love?

lines: Help me, my women! O, he's more mad
Than Telamon for his shield; the boar of Thessaly
Was never so embossed.
fullText: Help me, my women! O, he's more mad Than Telamon for his shield; the boar of Thessaly Was never so embossed.

lines: To th' monument!--
Mardian, go tell him I have slain myself.
Say that the last I spoke was "Antony,"
And word it, prithee, piteously. Hence, Mardian,
And bring me how he takes my death.--To th'
monument!
fullText: To th' monument!-- Mardian, go tell him I have slain myself. Say that the last I spoke was "Antony," And word it, prithee, piteously. Hence, Mardian, And bring me how he takes my death.--To th' monument!

lines: O Charmian, I will never go from hence.
fullText: O Charmian, I will never go from hence.

lines: No, I will not.
All strange and terrible events are welcome,
But comforts we despise. Our size of sorrow,
Proportioned to our cause, must be as great
As that which makes it.
fullText: No, I will not. All strange and terrible events are welcome, But comforts we despise. Our size of sorrow, Proportioned to our cause, must be as great As that which makes it.

lines: O sun,
Burn the great sphere thou mov'st in. Darkling stand
The varying shore o' th' world! O Antony, Antony,
Antony! Help, Charmian! Help, Iras, help!
Help, friends below! Let's draw him hither.
fullText: O sun, Burn the great sphere thou mov'st in. Darkling stand The varying shore o' th' world! O Antony, Antony, Antony! Help, Charmian! Help, Iras, help! Help, friends below! Let's draw him hither.

lines: So it should be that none but Antony
Should conquer Antony, but woe 'tis so!
fullText: So it should be that none but Antony Should conquer Antony, but woe 'tis so!

lines: I dare not, dear,
Dear my lord, pardon, I dare not,
Lest I be taken. Not th' imperious show
Of the full-fortuned Caesar ever shall
Be brooched with me; if knife, drugs, serpents have
Edge, sting, or operation, I am safe.
Your wife Octavia, with her modest eyes
And still conclusion, shall acquire no honor
Demuring upon me. But come, come, Antony.--
Help me, my women!--We must draw thee up.--
Assist, good friends.
fullText: I dare not, dear, Dear my lord, pardon, I dare not, Lest I be taken. Not th' imperious show Of the full-fortuned Caesar ever shall Be brooched with me; if knife, drugs, serpents have Edge, sting, or operation, I am safe. Your wife Octavia, with her modest eyes And still conclusion, shall acquire no honor Demuring upon me. But come, come, Antony.-- Help me, my women!--We must draw thee up.-- Assist, good friends.

lines: Here's sport indeed. How heavy weighs my lord!
Our strength is all gone into heaviness;
That makes the weight. Had I great Juno's power,
The strong-winged Mercury should fetch thee up
And set thee by Jove's side. Yet come a little.
Wishers were ever fools. O, come, come, come!
And welcome, welcome! Die when thou hast lived;
Quicken with kissing. Had my lips that power,
Thus would I wear them out.
fullText: Here's sport indeed. How heavy weighs my lord! Our strength is all gone into heaviness; That makes the weight. Had I great Juno's power, The strong-winged Mercury should fetch thee up And set thee by Jove's side. Yet come a little. Wishers were ever fools. O, come, come, come! And welcome, welcome! Die when thou hast lived; Quicken with kissing. Had my lips that power, Thus would I wear them out.

lines: No, let me speak, and let me rail so high
That the false huswife Fortune break her wheel,
Provoked by my offense.
fullText: No, let me speak, and let me rail so high That the false huswife Fortune break her wheel, Provoked by my offense.

lines: They do not go together.
fullText: They do not go together.

lines: My resolution and my hands I'll trust,
None about Caesar.
fullText: My resolution and my hands I'll trust, None about Caesar.

lines: Noblest of men, woo't die?
Hast thou no care of me? Shall I abide
In this dull world, which in thy absence is
No better than a sty? O see, my women,
The crown o' th' Earth doth melt.--My lord!
O, withered is the garland of the war;
The soldier's pole is fall'n; young boys and girls
Are level now with men. The odds is gone,
And there is nothing left remarkable
Beneath the visiting moon.
fullText: Noblest of men, woo't die? Hast thou no care of me? Shall I abide In this dull world, which in thy absence is No better than a sty? O see, my women, The crown o' th' Earth doth melt.--My lord! O, withered is the garland of the war; The soldier's pole is fall'n; young boys and girls Are level now with men. The odds is gone, And there is nothing left remarkable Beneath the visiting moon.

lines: No more but e'en a woman, and commanded
By such poor passion as the maid that milks
And does the meanest chares. It were for me
To throw my scepter at the injurious gods,
To tell them that this world did equal theirs
Till they had stolen our jewel. All's but naught.
Patience is sottish, and impatience does
Become a dog that's mad. Then is it sin
To rush into the secret house of death
Ere death dare come to us? How do you, women?
What, what, good cheer! Why, how now, Charmian?
My noble girls! Ah, women, women! Look,
Our lamp is spent; it's out. Good sirs, take heart.
We'll bury him; and then, what's brave, what's
noble,
Let's do 't after the high Roman fashion
And make death proud to take us. Come, away.
This case of that huge spirit now is cold.
Ah women, women! Come, we have no friend
But resolution and the briefest end.
fullText: No more but e'en a woman, and commanded By such poor passion as the maid that milks And does the meanest chares. It were for me To throw my scepter at the injurious gods, To tell them that this world did equal theirs Till they had stolen our jewel. All's but naught. Patience is sottish, and impatience does Become a dog that's mad. Then is it sin To rush into the secret house of death Ere death dare come to us? How do you, women? What, what, good cheer! Why, how now, Charmian? My noble girls! Ah, women, women! Look, Our lamp is spent; it's out. Good sirs, take heart. We'll bury him; and then, what's brave, what's noble, Let's do 't after the high Roman fashion And make death proud to take us. Come, away. This case of that huge spirit now is cold. Ah women, women! Come, we have no friend But resolution and the briefest end.

lines: My desolation does begin to make
A better life. 'Tis paltry to be Caesar;
Not being Fortune, he's but Fortune's knave,
A minister of her will. And it is great
To do that thing that ends all other deeds,
Which shackles accidents and bolts up change,
Which sleeps and never palates more the dung,
The beggar's nurse, and Caesar's.
fullText: My desolation does begin to make A better life. 'Tis paltry to be Caesar; Not being Fortune, he's but Fortune's knave, A minister of her will. And it is great To do that thing that ends all other deeds, Which shackles accidents and bolts up change, Which sleeps and never palates more the dung, The beggar's nurse, and Caesar's.

lines: What's thy name?
fullText: What's thy name?

lines: Antony
Did tell me of you, bade me trust you, but
I do not greatly care to be deceived
That have no use for trusting. If your master
Would have a queen his beggar, you must tell him
That majesty, to keep decorum, must
No less beg than a kingdom. If he please
To give me conquered Egypt for my son,
He gives me so much of mine own as I
Will kneel to him with thanks.
fullText: Antony Did tell me of you, bade me trust you, but I do not greatly care to be deceived That have no use for trusting. If your master Would have a queen his beggar, you must tell him That majesty, to keep decorum, must No less beg than a kingdom. If he please To give me conquered Egypt for my son, He gives me so much of mine own as I Will kneel to him with thanks.

lines: Pray you tell him
I am his fortune's vassal and I send him
The greatness he has got. I hourly learn
A doctrine of obedience, and would gladly
Look him i' th' face.
fullText: Pray you tell him I am his fortune's vassal and I send him The greatness he has got. I hourly learn A doctrine of obedience, and would gladly Look him i' th' face.

lines: Quick, quick, good hands!
fullText: Quick, quick, good hands!

lines: What, of death, too,
That rids our dogs of languish?
fullText: What, of death, too, That rids our dogs of languish?

lines: Where art thou, Death?
Come hither, come! Come, come, and take a queen
Worth many babes and beggars.
fullText: Where art thou, Death? Come hither, come! Come, come, and take a queen Worth many babes and beggars.

lines: Sir, I will eat no meat; I'll not drink, sir.
If idle talk will once be necessary--
I'll not sleep neither. This mortal house I'll ruin,
Do Caesar what he can. Know, sir, that I
Will not wait pinioned at your master's court,
Nor once be chastised with the sober eye
Of dull Octavia. Shall they hoist me up
And show me to the shouting varletry
Of censuring Rome? Rather a ditch in Egypt
Be gentle grave unto me; rather on Nilus' mud
Lay me stark naked, and let the waterflies
Blow me into abhorring; rather make
My country's high pyramides my gibbet
And hang me up in chains!
fullText: Sir, I will eat no meat; I'll not drink, sir. If idle talk will once be necessary-- I'll not sleep neither. This mortal house I'll ruin, Do Caesar what he can. Know, sir, that I Will not wait pinioned at your master's court, Nor once be chastised with the sober eye Of dull Octavia. Shall they hoist me up And show me to the shouting varletry Of censuring Rome? Rather a ditch in Egypt Be gentle grave unto me; rather on Nilus' mud Lay me stark naked, and let the waterflies Blow me into abhorring; rather make My country's high pyramides my gibbet And hang me up in chains!

lines: Say I would die.
fullText: Say I would die.

lines: I cannot tell.
fullText: I cannot tell.

lines: No matter, sir, what I have heard or known.
You laugh when boys or women tell their dreams;
Is 't not your trick?
fullText: No matter, sir, what I have heard or known. You laugh when boys or women tell their dreams; Is 't not your trick?

lines: I dreamt there was an emperor Antony.
O, such another sleep, that I might see
But such another man.
fullText: I dreamt there was an emperor Antony. O, such another sleep, that I might see But such another man.

lines: His face was as the heavens, and therein stuck
A sun and moon, which kept their course and
lighted
The little O, the Earth.
fullText: His face was as the heavens, and therein stuck A sun and moon, which kept their course and lighted The little O, the Earth.

lines: His legs bestrid the ocean, his reared arm
Crested the world. His voice was propertied
As all the tuned spheres, and that to friends;
But when he meant to quail and shake the orb,
He was as rattling thunder. For his bounty,
There was no winter in 't; an autumn 'twas
That grew the more by reaping. His delights
Were dolphin-like; they showed his back above
The element they lived in. In his livery
Walked crowns and crownets; realms and islands
were
As plates dropped from his pocket.
fullText: His legs bestrid the ocean, his reared arm Crested the world. His voice was propertied As all the tuned spheres, and that to friends; But when he meant to quail and shake the orb, He was as rattling thunder. For his bounty, There was no winter in 't; an autumn 'twas That grew the more by reaping. His delights Were dolphin-like; they showed his back above The element they lived in. In his livery Walked crowns and crownets; realms and islands were As plates dropped from his pocket.

lines: Think you there was, or might be, such a man
As this I dreamt of?
fullText: Think you there was, or might be, such a man As this I dreamt of?

lines: You lie up to the hearing of the gods!
But if there be nor ever were one such,
It's past the size of dreaming. Nature wants stuff
To vie strange forms with fancy, yet t' imagine
An Antony were nature's piece 'gainst fancy,
Condemning shadows quite.
fullText: You lie up to the hearing of the gods! But if there be nor ever were one such, It's past the size of dreaming. Nature wants stuff To vie strange forms with fancy, yet t' imagine An Antony were nature's piece 'gainst fancy, Condemning shadows quite.

lines: I thank you, sir.
Know you what Caesar means to do with me?
fullText: I thank you, sir. Know you what Caesar means to do with me?

lines: Nay, pray you, sir.
fullText: Nay, pray you, sir.

lines: He'll lead me, then, in triumph.
fullText: He'll lead me, then, in triumph.

lines: Sir, the gods
Will have it thus. My master and my lord
I must obey.
fullText: Sir, the gods Will have it thus. My master and my lord I must obey.

lines: Sole sir o' th' world,
I cannot project mine own cause so well
To make it clear, but do confess I have
Been laden with like frailties which before
Have often shamed our sex.
fullText: Sole sir o' th' world, I cannot project mine own cause so well To make it clear, but do confess I have Been laden with like frailties which before Have often shamed our sex.

lines: And may through all the world. 'Tis yours, and we,
Your scutcheons and your signs of conquest, shall
Hang in what place you please. Here, my good lord.
fullText: And may through all the world. 'Tis yours, and we, Your scutcheons and your signs of conquest, shall Hang in what place you please. Here, my good lord.

lines: This is the brief of money, plate, and jewels
I am possessed of. 'Tis exactly valued,
Not petty things admitted.--Where's Seleucus?
fullText: This is the brief of money, plate, and jewels I am possessed of. 'Tis exactly valued, Not petty things admitted.--Where's Seleucus?

lines: This is my treasurer. Let him speak, my lord,
Upon his peril, that I have reserved
To myself nothing.--Speak the truth, Seleucus.
fullText: This is my treasurer. Let him speak, my lord, Upon his peril, that I have reserved To myself nothing.--Speak the truth, Seleucus.

lines: What have I kept back?
fullText: What have I kept back?

lines: See, Caesar, O, behold
How pomp is followed! Mine will now be yours,
And should we shift estates, yours would be mine.
The ingratitude of this Seleucus does
Even make me wild.--O slave, of no more trust
Than love that's hired! What, goest thou back? Thou
shalt
Go back, I warrant thee! But I'll catch thine eyes
Though they had wings. Slave, soulless villain, dog!
O rarely base!
fullText: See, Caesar, O, behold How pomp is followed! Mine will now be yours, And should we shift estates, yours would be mine. The ingratitude of this Seleucus does Even make me wild.--O slave, of no more trust Than love that's hired! What, goest thou back? Thou shalt Go back, I warrant thee! But I'll catch thine eyes Though they had wings. Slave, soulless villain, dog! O rarely base!

lines: O Caesar, what a wounding shame is this,
That thou vouchsafing here to visit me,
Doing the honor of thy lordliness
To one so meek, that mine own servant should
Parcel the sum of my disgraces by
Addition of his envy! Say, good Caesar,
That I some lady trifles have reserved,
Immoment toys, things of such dignity
As we greet modern friends withal, and say
Some nobler token I have kept apart
For Livia and Octavia, to induce
Their mediation, must I be unfolded
With one that I have bred? The gods! It smites me
Beneath the fall I have. Prithee, go
hence,
Or I shall show the cinders of my spirits
Through th' ashes of my chance. Wert thou a man,
Thou wouldst have mercy on me.
fullText: O Caesar, what a wounding shame is this, That thou vouchsafing here to visit me, Doing the honor of thy lordliness To one so meek, that mine own servant should Parcel the sum of my disgraces by Addition of his envy! Say, good Caesar, That I some lady trifles have reserved, Immoment toys, things of such dignity As we greet modern friends withal, and say Some nobler token I have kept apart For Livia and Octavia, to induce Their mediation, must I be unfolded With one that I have bred? The gods! It smites me Beneath the fall I have. Prithee, go hence, Or I shall show the cinders of my spirits Through th' ashes of my chance. Wert thou a man, Thou wouldst have mercy on me.

lines: Be it known that we, the greatest, are misthought
For things that others do; and when we fall,
We answer others' merits in our name--
Are therefore to be pitied.
fullText: Be it known that we, the greatest, are misthought For things that others do; and when we fall, We answer others' merits in our name-- Are therefore to be pitied.

lines: My master and my lord!
fullText: My master and my lord!

lines: He words me, girls, he words me, that I should not
Be noble to myself. But hark thee, Charmian.
fullText: He words me, girls, he words me, that I should not Be noble to myself. But hark thee, Charmian.

lines: Hie thee again.
I have spoke already, and it is provided.
Go put it to the haste.
fullText: Hie thee again. I have spoke already, and it is provided. Go put it to the haste.

lines: Dolabella.
fullText: Dolabella.

lines: Dolabella,
I shall remain your debtor.
fullText: Dolabella, I shall remain your debtor.

lines: Farewell, and thanks.
Now, Iras, what think'st thou?
Thou an Egyptian puppet shall be shown
In Rome as well as I. Mechanic slaves
With greasy aprons, rules, and hammers shall
Uplift us to the view. In their thick breaths,
Rank of gross diet, shall we be enclouded
And forced to drink their vapor.
fullText: Farewell, and thanks. Now, Iras, what think'st thou? Thou an Egyptian puppet shall be shown In Rome as well as I. Mechanic slaves With greasy aprons, rules, and hammers shall Uplift us to the view. In their thick breaths, Rank of gross diet, shall we be enclouded And forced to drink their vapor.

lines: Nay, 'tis most certain, Iras. Saucy lictors
Will catch at us like strumpets, and scald rhymers
Ballad us out o' tune. The quick comedians
Extemporally will stage us and present
Our Alexandrian revels. Antony
Shall be brought drunken forth, and I shall see
Some squeaking Cleopatra boy my greatness
I' th' posture of a whore.
fullText: Nay, 'tis most certain, Iras. Saucy lictors Will catch at us like strumpets, and scald rhymers Ballad us out o' tune. The quick comedians Extemporally will stage us and present Our Alexandrian revels. Antony Shall be brought drunken forth, and I shall see Some squeaking Cleopatra boy my greatness I' th' posture of a whore.

lines: Nay, that's certain.
fullText: Nay, that's certain.

lines: Why, that's the way
To fool their preparation and to conquer
Their most absurd intents.
fullText: Why, that's the way To fool their preparation and to conquer Their most absurd intents.

lines: Let him come in.
What poor an instrument
May do a noble deed! He brings me liberty.
My resolution's placed, and I have nothing
Of woman in me. Now from head to foot
I am marble-constant. Now the fleeting moon
No planet is of mine.
fullText: Let him come in. What poor an instrument May do a noble deed! He brings me liberty. My resolution's placed, and I have nothing Of woman in me. Now from head to foot I am marble-constant. Now the fleeting moon No planet is of mine.

lines: Avoid, and leave him.
Hast thou the pretty worm of Nilus there
That kills and pains not?
fullText: Avoid, and leave him. Hast thou the pretty worm of Nilus there That kills and pains not?

lines: Remember'st thou any that have died on 't?
fullText: Remember'st thou any that have died on 't?

lines: Get thee hence. Farewell.
fullText: Get thee hence. Farewell.

lines: Farewell.
fullText: Farewell.

lines: Ay, ay, farewell.
fullText: Ay, ay, farewell.

lines: Take thou no care; it shall be heeded.
fullText: Take thou no care; it shall be heeded.

lines: Will it eat me?
fullText: Will it eat me?

lines: Well, get thee gone. Farewell.
fullText: Well, get thee gone. Farewell.

lines: Give me my robe. Put on my crown. I have
Immortal longings in me. Now no more
The juice of Egypt's grape shall moist this lip.
Yare, yare, good Iras, quick. Methinks I hear
Antony call. I see him rouse himself
To praise my noble act. I hear him mock
The luck of Caesar, which the gods give men
To excuse their after wrath.--Husband, I come!
Now to that name my courage prove my title.
I am fire and air; my other elements
I give to baser life.--So, have you done?
Come then, and take the last warmth of my lips.
Farewell, kind Charmian.--Iras, long farewell.
Have I the aspic in my lips? Dost fall?
If thou and nature can so gently part,
The stroke of death is as a lover's pinch,
Which hurts and is desired. Dost thou lie still?
If thus thou vanishest, thou tell'st the world
It is not worth leave-taking.
fullText: Give me my robe. Put on my crown. I have Immortal longings in me. Now no more The juice of Egypt's grape shall moist this lip. Yare, yare, good Iras, quick. Methinks I hear Antony call. I see him rouse himself To praise my noble act. I hear him mock The luck of Caesar, which the gods give men To excuse their after wrath.--Husband, I come! Now to that name my courage prove my title. I am fire and air; my other elements I give to baser life.--So, have you done? Come then, and take the last warmth of my lips. Farewell, kind Charmian.--Iras, long farewell. Have I the aspic in my lips? Dost fall? If thou and nature can so gently part, The stroke of death is as a lover's pinch, Which hurts and is desired. Dost thou lie still? If thus thou vanishest, thou tell'st the world It is not worth leave-taking.

lines: This proves me base.
If she first meet the curled Antony,
He'll make demand of her, and spend that kiss
Which is my heaven to have.--Come, thou mortal
wretch,
With thy sharp teeth this knot intrinsicate
Of life at once untie. Poor venomous fool,
Be angry and dispatch. O, couldst thou speak,
That I might hear thee call great Caesar ass
Unpolicied!
fullText: This proves me base. If she first meet the curled Antony, He'll make demand of her, and spend that kiss Which is my heaven to have.--Come, thou mortal wretch, With thy sharp teeth this knot intrinsicate Of life at once untie. Poor venomous fool, Be angry and dispatch. O, couldst thou speak, That I might hear thee call great Caesar ass Unpolicied!

lines: Peace, peace!
Dost thou not see my baby at my breast,
That sucks the nurse asleep?
fullText: Peace, peace! Dost thou not see my baby at my breast, That sucks the nurse asleep?

lines: As sweet as balm, as soft as air, as gentle--
O Antony!--Nay, I will take thee too.
What should I stay--
fullText: As sweet as balm, as soft as air, as gentle-- O Antony!--Nay, I will take thee too. What should I stay--
